---
title: "Datasets"
---

This page is your one-stop reference for getting set up: the data, the file structure, the packages, and where the source code lives. For step-by-step download instructions and troubleshooting, see [Accessing the data and setting up R](lab_setup.qmd).

## R packages required

Install these before the lab session:

```r
install.packages(c(
  # core spatial
  "sf",          # vector spatial data
  "terra",       # raster operations (modern replacement for raster)

  # data wrangling
  "tidyverse",   # dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, purrr, etc.
  "readxl",      # read the survey .xlsx file
  "here",        # project-relative file paths

  # mapping
  "tmap",        # thematic + interactive maps
  "ggspatial",   # scale bar, north arrow, basemap tiles
  "ggrepel",     # non-overlapping map labels
  "classInt",    # break classification for choropleths
  "viridis",     # accessible colour palettes
  "knitr"        # tables
))
```

## Datasets

The lab uses **four data sources**, each measuring damage from a different angle. Files are loaded directly from `data/` paths in the Quarto chunks.

### 1. Satellite damage at zone level

**File:** `data/geo/raster_rf_damage.shp` (with `.dbf`, `.shx`, `.prj` siblings)

The output of the pipeline described in §1.4 of Part 1. 139 operational-zone polygons covering greater Beirut, each carrying the share of \~0.5 m pixels classified as severely damaged by a supervised Random Forest applied to pre/post MAXAR WorldView imagery.

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `zon_nmb` → `zone_number` | Operational zone identifier (renamed in code) |
| `Cdstr_1` → `cadastre` | Cadastre name (renamed in code) |
| `pct___2` → `pct_severe_adj` | % pixels severely damaged (renamed in code) |
| `geometry` | Polygon |

::: callout-note
The original column names in the shapefile are truncated to 10 characters (a shapefile format limitation). The labs rename them in the load step for readability.
:::

### 2. MAXAR pre/post imagery (Part 1 §1.5 only)

**File:** `data/maxar_processed/MX_31Jul_5Aug_UN.tif`

The UN-SPIDER / HOT pre-processed composite — orthorectified, atmospherically compensated, and pansharpened. Three RGB bands per acquisition, \~0.5 m resolution. Only used to display the before/after visual in §1.5; the rest of the lab works from the pre-computed zonal output above.

### 3. Building-level damage assessments

**File:** `data/geo/buildings_damaged.shp`

Every building in central Beirut assessed by **Copernicus EMSN087** ([JRC product page](https://data.jrc.ec.europa.eu/dataset/aa4e3619-4e53-406d-8dec-954d9a6372d3)) after the blast. Each building has two parallel damage grades.

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `bld_nbr` | Unique building ID (joins to the survey) |
| `dmg_grd` | Damage grade from Copernicus EMSN087 photointerpretation |
| `dmg_JRC` | Alternative grade from the JRC automated pipeline |
| `geometry` | Building footprint polygon |

`dmg_grd` is the expert-analyst grading (categories: *no damage*, *minor damage*, *moderate damage*, *severe damage*, *destroyed*, *under reconstruction*, *repaired*, *new building*). `dmg_JRC` is a separate automated classification (*No visible damage*, *Possible damage*, *Damaged*, *Destroyed*, etc., plus *Not analysed*).

### 4. Synthetic household survey

**File:** `data/kr_rb_mm_synthetic.xlsx`

A synthetic household survey spanning three Beirut neighbourhoods (**Karantina**, **Mar Mikhael**, **Ras Beirut**) in two waves (2018 baseline, 2021 post-blast). Used in Part 2 from §2.4 onwards.

| Variable | Description | Coding |
|---|---|---|
| `bld_nbr` | Building ID (joins to buildings layer) | — |
| `neighbourhood` | One of the three sampled neighbourhoods | — |
| `year` | Survey wave | 2018 / 2021 |
| `windows_rev` | Self-reported damage to windows | 1 = severe → 4 = none *(flipped in load)* |
| `walls_rev` | Self-reported damage to walls | 1 = severe → 4 = none *(flipped in load)* |
| `furniture_rev` | Self-reported damage to furniture | 1 = severe → 4 = none *(flipped in load)* |
| `BLASTav` | Average of the three above | 1–4 continuous *(flipped in load)* |
| `rel_blast` | Was anyone in the household harmed? | 0 / 1 |
| `struct` | Self-reported structural integrity | 1–4 ordinal |

::: callout-important
## The survey data is synthetic

For privacy reasons, only the *blast damage variables* (the six listed above) are preserved as collected. Everything else in the file — income, tenure, education, mental health — has been re-sampled. Marginal distributions and the link to building damage are kept; joint structure between non-blast variables is not. **Do not use this dataset for inferential analysis beyond the lab.**
:::

::: callout-warning
## The damage variables are inverted in the raw file

`windows_rev`, `walls_rev`, `furniture_rev` and `BLASTav` are coded 1 = severe, 4 = none in the raw `.xlsx`. The load step in §2.4 flips them with `5 - x` so the rest of the lab reads "higher = more damage". If you skip the flip, every chart, map and regression will read backwards.
:::

## Getting the files

The data lives in a Dropbox folder linked from [lab_setup.qmd](lab_setup.qmd). All code (the `.qmd` files and any updates) is on the course site at [pietrostefani.github.io/man-made-disasters](https://pietrostefani.github.io/man-made-disasters/).

::: callout-tip
## Don't clone the whole repository

If you only want the lab files, use the per-folder download method described in `lab_setup.qmd` rather than pulling the whole repo. The full clone includes rendered HTML output that will conflict with your own `.qmd` renders.
:::
